channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Trump-backed prayer festival on National Mall draws thousands: ‘We welcome Jesus!’
item.description: Critics of Sunday's "Rededicate 250" event said it portrayed the United States inaccurately as a Christian nation and sought to erase the line between church and state.

item.title: These secret government investigations fly in the face of the Constitution
item.description: These secret government investigations fly in the face of the Constitution

item.title: The woman who keeps beating men at America’s most punishing running events
item.description: Fueled by mashed potatoes and moxie, Rachel Entrekin blew past the field at a 250-mile race known for breaking competitors’ will.

item.title: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.
item.description: Still need to finish this weekend’s On the Record? We’ve got you.

item.title: Carolyn Hax: Husband abandoned their family but wants control of the narrative
item.description: Husband calls leaving their family and moving away “far more complicated” than the letter writer has explained to others.

item.title: Larry Hogan hoped Republicans would ‘get back to sanity.’ He’s lost faith.
item.description: The former governor of Maryland is launching a leadership institute at Washington College and says he's done with seeking political office.

item.title: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now
item.description: Crossword and a coffee: Solve the Sunday puzzle now

item.title: Bill Cassidy’s defeat highlights America’s increasingly broken primary system
item.description: Bill Cassidy and America’s increasingly broken primary system

item.title: AI license plate cameras tore this town apart and led to a state of emergency
item.description: In Troy, New York, residents and city officials are at odds over police use of Flock cameras, which some call a safety tool and others see as surveillance.

item.title: GOP Sen. Bill Cassidy of Louisiana, who voted to convict Trump in his 2021 impeachment trial, loses primary
item.description: The two-term senator, one of the few remaining lawmakers who broke with Trump after the U.S. Capital attack, failed to advance to the runoff in Louisiana’s GOP Senate primary after voters heeded the president’s call to oust him. Rep. Julia Letlow, whom Trump endorsed, and John Fleming, the state treasurer, were projected to advance.

item.title: 5 foods to support strong bones
item.description: Eating the right nutrients can help you in the fight to maintain bone mass as you age.

item.title: Women with this condition are often misdiagnosed. Its new name could help.
item.description: The PCOS name change aims to reflect the full scope of the condition — and finally unlock the care women have been seeking for years.

item.title: His mother warned him to be careful. By evening, the D.C. student was dying.
item.description: The fatal shooting of a star high school football player has left a D.C. neighborhood on edge.

item.title: He’s king of the AI boom. Why do former colleagues say he can’t be trusted?
item.description: Testimony in a case brought by Elon Musk has given new force to persistent questions about the trustworthiness of OpenAI’s CEO Sam Altman.

item.title: Virginia’s new House map is dead. Democrats think they can still flip these seats.
item.description: Republicans won the redistricting war in Virginia, but several lawmakers remain vulnerable.

item.title: Trump’s China summit shows toll of a difficult year for the president
item.description: A year ago, President Donald Trump promised a new era during the first major foreign trip of his second term. On his recent visit to Beijing, the war with Iran and economic strain clouded his diplomacy.

item.title: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.
item.description: How many guesses will Keyword take you today? Try to beat the average now.

item.title: She was deported without her toddler. ICE blamed her for his killing.
item.description: ICE accused Wendy Hernandez Reyes of leaving her child with a violent uncle, but she says her son would still be alive if officers hadn’t detained and deported her. A review of court records, along with Hernandez’s account, contradicts ICE’s narrative and raises questions about how the Trump administration is deporting scores of parents, many without their children.

item.title: Supreme Court blocks effort to revive Virginia voting map that bolsters Democrats
item.description: The high court’s decision is the latest blow to Democrats amid a nationwide redistricting war in which Republicans have seized the upper hand in recent weeks.

item.title: 2020 election denier Tina Peters granted clemency by Democratic governor, potentially shaving years off her prison sentence
item.description: Colorado Gov. Jared Polis (D) had been under pressure from President Donald Trump to pardon Peters, a former county clerk who helped secretly copy voting machines’ hard drives.

item.title: 5 graduation speeches with truly helpful tips for living your life
item.description: Every spring, commencement speakers offer their wisdom. We combed through dozens of speeches from over the years and pulled out some of the best advice.

item.title: The UFO files reveal a hidden truth about a growing religion
item.description: Belief in alien life is on the rise — fueled by declining trust in institutions, Diana Walsh Pasulka writes.

item.title: Parents of teens who break curfew in D.C. will be prosecuted, DOJ says
item.description: The federal crackdown on crime comes ahead of 250th anniversary events in the nation's capital.

item.title: DOJ will seek death penalty for suspect in fatal shooting of couple at D.C. Jewish museum
item.description: In a court filing, prosecutors said the suspect in the May 2025 shooting at the Capital Jewish Museum was “motivated by political, ideological, national, and religious bias, contempt, and hatred.”

item.title: Carolyn Hax chat: I turned 18, and I'm leaving my parents. Do I tell them?
item.description: Our advice columnist answers your questions about the strange train we call life.

item.title: My world ended on Feb. 24, 2022. Now this warning is for you.
item.description: Russia knows it doesn't have to win on the battlefield if it can break people at home.

item.title: The Smithsonian’s most contested exhibition is back on view, mostly intact, our columnist writes
item.description: According to critic Philip Kennicott, the National Portrait Gallery curators of the “America’s Presidents” exhibit have found a way to counter the Trump administration: with facts.

item.title: Wager your points and finish off the week with today’s On the Record
item.description: Wager your points and finish off the week with today’s On the Record.

item.title: Trump announces D.C’s West Potomac Park as site of sculpture garden. It is unclear whether congressional approval would be needed.
item.description: Any new memorial project in West Potomac Park, such as President Donald Trump's proposed "National Garden of American Heroes," would probably require congressional approval as well as review by federal planning bodies.

item.title: In Ukraine, it was boy vs. Russian drone. The boy won.
item.description: A soldier taught a 12-year-old how to disable the fiber-optic drones that Russia has been using to hunt Ukrainian civilians in a campaign the U.N. has labeled a war crime.

item.title: Trump-Xi talks. Abortion pills. Stock deadline. ICE warehouses. Political influencers. Storms. Grad speeches. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: RFK Jr. is going to war against SSRIs. Doctors and patients are caught in the middle.
item.description: After years in the shadows, doctors and health officials are reckoning with the challenges of getting off antidepressants.

item.title: Supreme Court temporarily clears way for mail distribution of widely used abortion pill
item.description: The decision will, for now, preserve access nationwide to medication abortion, the most common way of ending pregnancies in the United States.

item.title: What brought down Stephen Colbert’s ‘Late Show’
item.description: What brought down Stephen Colbert’s ‘Late Show’

item.title: ICE moving forward with warehouse detention plan despite lawsuits, investigation
item.description: New warehouse contracts in Texas are expected to be awarded after Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin initially called for a review of the plan.

item.title: Eat early, skip the steak and more lessons from dining out seven nights a week
item.description: Post food critic Elazar Sontag shares his takeaways from his first months of breakneck dining in Washington.

item.title: DOJ says Yale medical school discriminated against Asian and White applicants
item.description: A Justice Department investigation concluded that Yale School of Medicine discriminated based on race in its admissions, favoring Black and Hispanic applicants over White and Asian ones.

item.title: Trump’s ballroom rises aboveground, but the legal battle isn’t over
item.description: Crews have started building the ground floor that will hold a kitchen and offices for the first lady, even as a court battle and funding fight threaten the project.

item.title: Rubio is banned from China, but Beijing may have found a way around it
item.description: Chinese officials are using a different transliterated character for the secretary of state’s name, perhaps to allow him to visit without lifting the 2020 ban.

item.title: Here are the undergraduate programs that pay off (and some that don’t)
item.description: Going to college works out financially for most students, a report shows, but the benefits depend greatly on the types of programs they enroll in.

item.title: Has the U.S. lost the Iran war? Watch ‘Make It Make Sense.’
item.description: The latest from "Make It Make Sense" on the war in Iran.

item.title: Traveling with CEOs in today’s On the Record
item.description: Traveling with CEOs in today’s On the Record

item.title: White House planned to work on 250-foot arch near Arlington National Cemetery under unrelated contract, bypassing bidding process
item.description: The unusual move would allow the administration to sidestep a public bidding process, relying on a deal for engineering services already underway across the Potomac River at the White House.

item.title: Trump in China. Fed chair. Trump’s arch. Alex Murdaugh. College degrees. Heart health. Neanderthal dentist. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: White House to host 9-hour prayer festival focused on Christian roots of U.S.
item.description: The Trump administration is hosting an all-day prayer festival on the National Mall on Sunday that organizers say will reflect the country’s Christian origins and, they hope, spark “a movement of renewal” in America. Pete Hegseth, Marco Rubio and Mike Johnson will speak at the event.

item.title: House set to launch bipartisan push to combat sexual misconduct in Congress
item.description: House Speaker Mike Johnson and Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries are announcing a task force “to make Congress a safer work environment” following several high-profile cases.

item.title: China gains major edge on U.S. amid Iran war, intelligence report finds
item.description: A confidential assessment, circulating as President Donald Trump begins his highly anticipated trip to Beijing, shows shifts in several key areas of competition.

item.title: Who will win the House? The scales are shifting.
item.description: Who will win the House? The scales are shifting.

item.title: Senate confirms new Fed chair as Trump allies warn rate cuts may not happen this year
item.description: Kevin Warsh promised to remake the central bank. His first challenge: inflation that will make the president's demand for interest rate cuts impossible to meet.

item.title: Senate rejects bill to halt Iran war despite growing frustrations
item.description: It was the first such vote since a deadline passed for the Trump administration to seek Congress’s approval to continue the military campaign.
